headline: Montana Author and Historian Michael Andrew Nistler Born, 1952
body:
Now, if you've ever spent a spell out in that grand, sprawling territory they call Montana, you'll know it's a place where history ain't just written in books, it's etched into the very landscape, whispered on the wind, and carried in the tales of its folk. And it was into this very spirit, on a warm July 12th back in '52, that a fellow by the name of Michael Andrew Nistler first drew breath in Glendive, way out yonder in Dawson County. He wasn't born with a silver spoon, nor with a grand title, but he was born with a keen eye and a listening ear, which, in a land like Montana, are worth more than all the gold in the gulches.

Young Nistler, as he grew, took to the stories of his home like a trout to a clear mountain stream. He understood, you see, that the true wealth of Montana wasn't just in its copper or its cattle, but in the grit and gumption of the pioneers, the quiet endurance of the homesteaders, and the rich, often untold, sagas of eastern Montana. He dedicated his life, not to chasing fortunes, but to corralling those fleeting narratives, to pinning down the truth of what it meant to carve out a life in a place that could be both bountiful and brutally unforgiving. He aimed to make sure that the struggles and triumphs, the laughter and the tears of those who came before, wouldn't just fade away like a prairie sunset.

His early years, though, were not without their trials. Before he was even three, little Mike wrestled with high fevers, infections, and seizures, leaving him with a moderate cognitive impairment. But if you knew Mike, you'd know that adversity, like a stubborn mule, only made him dig in his heels. He was, by all accounts, a happy, positive, and helpful lad, always ready with a courteous word. He played whiffle ball with the vigor of a young colt, rode his bike with the wind in his hair, and spent his days outdoors with his siblings, soaking up the very essence of the land he would later champion.

At the tender age of twelve, his family pulled up stakes and moved to Plentywood, a town that would become the very heart of his existence. But fate, being the mischievous scamp it often is, wasn't done with him. Less than two years later, another bout of high fevers, this time rheumatic, laid him low. Yet, through it all, Mike Nistler remained an optimist, a man who refused to let the storms of life, be they illness or hardship, steal his joy. Even when the grim diagnosis of pancreatic cancer arrived in 2023, he faced it with a courage that would make a lesser man quail.

Now, Mike wasn't just a scholar of history, he was a participant in the grand theater of life. He was a natural athlete, a fact borne out by the eighty medals he earned competing in the Special Olympics. He never boasted, mind you, about his accomplishments, for humility was as much a part of him as his love for Montana. He was an independent soul, making his home in Plentywood, and becoming a fervent devotee of all Wildcat sports. You could find him, rain or shine, cheering for the orange and black, his voice ringing out for the Montana Grizzlies, Washington football, Laker basketball, and even those Chicago Cubs, a testament to his wide-ranging enthusiasms.

His connections ran deeper than mere blood. As a client of Glenwood since its very beginning in 1976, his truest friendships were forged with the clients and employees there. They were a community, a family, who befriended, respected, and supported him, and for whom he cared with a profound depth. He was, as one might say, a pillar of his community, a man who understood the value of a good story and the importance of remembering where you came from.

Uncle Mike, as he was affectionately known, was a staunch supporter of his seven nephews and five nieces, keeping in touch with frequent phone calls, a habit that speaks volumes about his devotion to family. He was preceded in death by his parents, Richard and Teresa, his brother Pat, his brothers-in-law Harold Ming and Paul Sandoval, and his sister-in-law Danette Nistler. But his legacy lives on through his surviving brothers, Joe, Larry, and Lou (Jeanne) Nistler, and his sisters, Geralyn (Alan) Jones, Marilyn (Casey) Elm, Carolyn (Kevin) Guenthner, and Lynette (Jeff) Ekness. They, like all who knew him, were heartbroken by his passing, yet eternally grateful for the wonderful gift he was to them, and to Montana. As the Plentywood Herald once wrote,
